text: This video presents the surgical technique for an endoscopic assisted extra-elevator abdoperineal excision or ELAP. An eighty-seven years old male patient presented a distal rectal cancer located at the level of the anorectal junction. He had an ASA score of three and a BMI of twenty-nine. The CT scan did not show any evidence of distant metastasis. In addition, the MRI of the pelvis showed a tumor, stage MT-NN, located at the level of the anorectal junction. Of note, the CRM was not treated. Finally, the patient underwent a preoperative radiochemotherapy with This is the MRI showing with an arrow the tumor location just at the anorectal junction.

The procedure begins with the abdominal phase using a laparoscopic setup, including a 12mm camera and three 5mm ports. A medial to lateral dissection is performed with high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) using Hemolock clips. The total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection proceeds anteriorly to the vesicles and posteriorly to the Valderia fascia. The ureters and hypogastric nerves are identified and preserved. The peritoneum is dissected on the medial side, and the left ureter is preserved. The peritoneum on the lateral side of the rectum is opened with a hook, and the TME plane is followed to the Valderia fascia. The anterior peritoneum is opened, revealing the vesicles. The narrow pelvis and patient's obesity make the dissection challenging.
The video displays the extent of the abdominal dissection achieved during the procedure.

The surgeon begins the perineal phase by closing the anus and performing an extra-sphincteric dissection. The skin is opened using a diathermy, and a Lone Star retractor is applied for optimal exposure. A running suture is placed on the skin edge for sealing before inserting the gel point port. Pneumopelvis is initiated with a pressure of twelve. Dissection starts posteriorly at the coccyx, following the TME plane, and continues with transection of the pelvic floor and right-side muscles according to ELAP principles.

The surgeon follows the TME plane cranially, joining the previous dissection. The levator muscles on the left side are divided using a hook. Anterior dissection is performed with caution to avoid injury to the prostate and nerves, while managing the risk of bleeding. The abdominal team assists by retracting the specimen with a suction device. Lateral attachments are divided using ligature, freeing the specimen for extraction through the anus with an Alexi wound protector. Hemostasis is checked, a washout is performed, and a routine drain is placed in the pelvis. An end colostomy is formed, and the perineal wound is closed in layers.
